questions.prompt: Why minimize signup form fields (email + password vs 12 fields)?

choices.text: Each form field reduces signup conversion measurably (3-10% per field is a common rough estimate). The user trades effort for hypothetical value; if effort is high before they've experienced any value, they bounce. Email-only signup with deferred profile completion (ask for name, role, company AFTER they've experienced the product) significantly outperforms front-loaded forms in conversion. Google's research and Optimizely / VWO case studies repeatedly show this. Better patterns: magic links (email only — click link to log in, no password yet), social login (Sign in with Google = one click, no form), progressive profiling (ask 1-2 things per session as needed rather than all upfront). The questions you SHOULD ask early are the ones that meaningfully personalize the experience (role, use case if it changes the UI) — at most 2-3. Everything else defers. Friction at signup compounds because that's the moment of highest 'should I even do this' doubt; reduce it.

questions.explanation: Each field reduces conversion 3-10%. Defer profile data until after value is demonstrated. Use magic links / social login.

questions.prompt: Your B2B SaaS has 20% signup → activation. What's the highest-leverage fix?

choices.text: Improving activation rate — going from 20% → 40% doubles your effective customer base for the same marketing spend; CAC effectively halves. To find the leverage points: instrument the funnel (Amplitude / Mixpanel / PostHog) to see where users drop between signup and activation; user-test the flow live (watch new signups on Zoom / via session replay tools like FullStory); ask churned users why ('what made you stop using it?'). Common findings at 20% activation: time-to-value too long (hours of setup); core feature not discoverable; collaboration features need teammates but invite flow is awkward; empty states give no direction; no email follow-up to re-engage. Each fix tested + measured. Compared to spending the same engineering effort on new features: improving the gate from signup to activation has higher ROI in most SaaS at this stage. After activation rate is reasonable (40-60%+), then optimize retention; then conversion to paid; then expansion. Each stage compounds with the prior. The 'just add more marketing' answer ignores that pouring more signups into a 20% activation funnel just wastes more marketing dollars.

questions.explanation: Improving activation compounds. 20%→40% doubles customer base; same marketing spend halves effective CAC.
